questionText: In a centralized mutual exclusion algorithm, how many messages are required for one entry into and exit from the critical section (request, grant, release)?
explanation: The centralized algorithm uses a coordinator: the client sends a request, receives a grant, and later sends a release — 3 messages per critical-section use. This is the lowest message count among the classic algorithms, at the cost of a single point of failure.
distractorRationales: 2 messages would omit either the grant or the release.
1 message cannot cover request, grant, and release.
2(n-1) is the message count of Ricart–Agrawala, a fully distributed algorithm, not the centralized one.

questionText: The Ricart–Agrawala distributed mutual exclusion algorithm requires how many messages per critical-section entry in a system of n processes?
explanation: Each requesting process sends a REQUEST to the other n-1 processes and must collect n-1 REPLY messages before entering, giving 2(n-1) messages per entry. It avoids a coordinator but generates far more traffic than the centralized scheme.
distractorRationales: 3 is the centralized algorithm's count, not the fully distributed Ricart–Agrawala.
n undercounts; both a request and a reply are exchanged with each of the n-1 peers.
There is no logarithmic factor; communication is with all peers.

questionText: In Ricart–Agrawala, process A (request timestamp 4) and process B (request timestamp 6) both want the critical section, and each receives the other's request. Who enters first, and what does the other do?
options: A enters first; B defers its reply to A until A finishes
B enters first because it requested with a larger timestamp
Both enter simultaneously since timestamps differ
Neither enters; the tie causes deadlock
explanation: Ricart–Agrawala grants priority to the lower timestamp. A (timestamp 4) outranks B (timestamp 6), so B sends its REPLY to A immediately while deferring its own entry; A replies to B only after leaving the critical section. A enters first.
distractorRationales: Lower timestamps win, so the larger-timestamp B yields, not the reverse.
Mutual exclusion forbids simultaneous entry; one must defer.
There is no deadlock; the timestamp ordering breaks the contention cleanly.

questionText: A token ring mutual exclusion algorithm passes a single token around a logical ring. Its main correctness advantage is that mutual exclusion is guaranteed because:
options: Only the holder of the unique token may enter the critical section
explanation: There is exactly one token; possession of it is the sole permission to enter the critical section. Since only one process holds the token at a time, mutual exclusion follows directly. The challenges are lost tokens and detecting a dead token holder.
distractorRationales: No voting occurs; the token alone confers access.
There is no central coordinator in a token ring; control circulates with the token.
Timestamps are used by Ricart–Agrawala, not by the token-ring scheme.

questionText: Which of the following is a key disadvantage of the centralized mutual exclusion algorithm?
options: The coordinator is a single point of failure and a bottleneck
explanation: Centralizing control in one coordinator makes it both a performance bottleneck and a single point of failure: if it crashes, no process can enter the critical section until a new coordinator is elected. It is otherwise very message-efficient (3 messages).
distractorRationales: 2(n-1) is Ricart–Agrawala's cost; the centralized algorithm uses only 3 messages.
It does correctly guarantee mutual exclusion via the coordinator.
It uses logical request ordering at the coordinator, not synchronized physical clocks.

questionText: In the Bully election algorithm, when a process notices the coordinator has failed, it starts an election by sending ELECTION messages to:
options: All processes with higher IDs than itself
All processes with lower IDs than itself
explanation: In the Bully algorithm a process sends ELECTION only to higher-numbered processes. If any responds, the initiator drops out; if none respond, it wins and announces itself as coordinator. The highest-ID live process always 'bullies' its way to coordinator.
distractorRationales: Lower-ID processes cannot outrank the initiator, so contacting them is pointless for winning.
The coordinator has failed; messaging only it accomplishes nothing.
Messaging everyone is wasteful; only higher IDs can beat the initiator.

questionText: In a Bully election among processes with IDs 1..7 where process 7 (the coordinator) has crashed and process 4 detects it and starts an election, which process becomes the new coordinator?
explanation: Process 4 messages 5, 6, 7. Processes 5 and 6 respond and start their own elections; 7 is dead. Eventually 6 messages only 7 (dead), gets no response, and declares itself coordinator since it is the highest live ID. The new coordinator is 6.
distractorRationales: Process 4 is superseded once higher-ID processes 5 and 6 respond.
Process 5 is beaten by 6, which has a higher ID and also responds.
Process 1 has the lowest ID and cannot win against any higher live process.

questionText: The Ring election algorithm circulates an ELECTION message containing process IDs around the ring. The eventual coordinator is the process with the:
options: Highest ID seen in the circulated list
Lowest ID seen in the circulated list
ID closest to the failed coordinator's ID
explanation: Each active process appends its ID to the ELECTION message as it circulates. When the message returns to the initiator, the highest ID in the collected list is chosen as coordinator, and a COORDINATOR message announces the result around the ring.
distractorRationales: The convention selects the maximum ID, not the minimum.
The initiator only starts the process; it wins only if it happens to be highest.
Proximity to the old coordinator's ID is irrelevant to the selection.

questionText: Which two safety/liveness properties must any correct mutual exclusion algorithm satisfy?
options: Safety (at most one process in the CS) and liveness (requests eventually granted)
explanation: Mutual exclusion correctness requires safety — never more than one process in the critical section — and liveness/progress — every request is eventually granted (no starvation if fairness is added). The other pairs describe transactions, not mutual exclusion.
distractorRationales: Atomicity and durability are ACID transaction properties, not mutual exclusion properties.
Idempotency and commutativity concern operations, not CS access control.
Serializability and isolation are database concurrency concepts.

questionText: A weakness of the Bully algorithm compared to the Ring algorithm is that, in the worst case, the number of messages it can generate is on the order of:
explanation: In the worst case — the lowest-ID process detects the failure — O(n^2) messages can be exchanged as each process in turn launches elections to all higher-ID processes. The Ring algorithm is more predictable at O(n) (or 2n) messages.
distractorRationales: O(n) is the Ring algorithm's bound, not the Bully worst case.
There is no logarithmic structure; it is all-to-higher messaging.
A constant number cannot elect a leader among n processes.

questionText: In a quorum-based (e.g., Maekawa's) mutual exclusion algorithm, a process must obtain permission from a voting set (quorum). The key design requirement on any two quorums is that they:
options: Must intersect (share at least one common member)
explanation: Maekawa's algorithm guarantees mutual exclusion because any two quorums share at least one member; that common member grants permission to only one requester at a time, so two processes cannot both collect full quorums simultaneously. Quorum size is about √n.
distractorRationales: Disjoint quorums would allow two processes to be granted access at once, breaking safety.
Requiring all n members would reduce to the costly all-to-all approach.
A single-process quorum reduces to a centralized scheme, losing the quorum advantage.

questionText: Maekawa's quorum-based algorithm achieves a message complexity per critical-section entry of approximately:
explanation: By organizing processes so each quorum has about √n members, Maekawa's algorithm needs roughly 3√n messages (request, reply, release with each quorum member) per entry — better than Ricart–Agrawala's 2(n-1).
distractorRationales: O(n) is Ricart–Agrawala's order; Maekawa improves on it.
O(n^2) is the Bully worst case, unrelated to quorum mutual exclusion.
Quorum sizes scale as √n, not logarithmically.

questionText: What problem can occur in a token-ring mutual exclusion algorithm that does NOT occur in the centralized algorithm?
options: Loss of the token requires a regeneration/recovery mechanism
explanation: If the single token is lost (e.g., the holder crashes), no process can enter the critical section until the token is regenerated, which requires detecting the loss and electing a regenerator. The centralized algorithm has no token to lose.
distractorRationales: Only the token holder may enter, so simultaneous permission cannot happen.
There is no coordinator in a token ring; that is the centralized algorithm's issue.
The token ring does not rely on timestamps at all.

questionText: Ricart–Agrawala uses Lamport timestamps to order competing requests. Why is breaking ties by process ID essential?
options: To impose a total order so that two equal-timestamp requests cannot both be deferred (deadlock-free)
To reduce the message count to O(log n)
To synchronize physical clocks across processes
To allow more than one process into the critical section
explanation: If two requests carry equal Lamport timestamps, a tie-break by process ID produces a strict total order, ensuring exactly one request has priority. Without it, both could defer to each other and deadlock. Tie-breaking does not change message complexity.
distractorRationales: Tie-breaking does not alter the 2(n-1) message complexity.
It orders requests logically; it does not synchronize physical clocks.
It preserves mutual exclusion — it never admits two processes at once.

questionText: In the Bully algorithm, after a recovered process with the highest ID rejoins the system, what does it do?
options: It starts an election and, finding no higher process, takes over as coordinator
It permanently defers to the current coordinator
It waits to be granted the coordinator role by a vote
It is barred from ever becoming coordinator again
explanation: The 'bully' behavior means a recovered highest-ID process immediately holds an election; since no live process outranks it, it wins and announces itself as the new coordinator, displacing the incumbent. This is the source of the algorithm's name.
distractorRationales: The highest-ID process does not defer; it asserts itself.
No collective vote is needed; the highest live ID simply wins.
Recovery does not disqualify a process; it can reclaim coordinator status.

questionText: The purpose of a leader (coordinator) election algorithm in a distributed system is to:
options: Have all processes agree on exactly one process to play a special role
Ensure mutual exclusion for shared variables
explanation: Election algorithms let a set of processes reach agreement on a single coordinator/leader (e.g., to act as a lock manager or sequencer) after the previous one fails. Mutual exclusion, clock sync, and message recovery are separate concerns.
distractorRationales: Mutual exclusion is a related but distinct problem; election chooses who coordinates it.
Clock synchronization is handled by NTP/Berkeley/Cristian, not election.
Lost-message recovery is a reliability concern, not the goal of election.

questionText: A distributed mutual exclusion algorithm is said to be 'fair' if requests are granted in the order they were made (by logical timestamp). Which classic algorithm provides this fairness guarantee directly?
options: Centralized with a simple FIFO-less coordinator
explanation: Ricart–Agrawala orders entries strictly by Lamport timestamp (with ID tie-break), so the earliest logical request is served first — a direct fairness guarantee. A token ring serves by ring position, not request time, and a coordinator's fairness depends on its queue discipline.
distractorRationales: The token ring grants by ring order, independent of when requests were made.
A coordinator without a FIFO/priority queue does not guarantee request-order fairness.
Random backoff is explicitly non-deterministic and unfair by timestamp.

questionText: A subtle problem in quorum-based mutual exclusion (Maekawa) that does not arise in Ricart–Agrawala is:
options: Deadlock, because a process may hold some votes while waiting for others
Inability to ever grant the critical section
explanation: Because each process can grant its vote to only one requester at a time, requesters may each hold a subset of votes and wait on each other, causing deadlock. Maekawa's algorithm therefore adds extra messages (INQUIRE/YIELD/FAILED) to detect and resolve this. Mutual exclusion itself remains safe.
distractorRationales: Mutual exclusion is preserved by quorum intersection; safety is not the issue.
There is no central coordinator with growing memory in Maekawa's scheme.
The critical section can be granted; deadlock is a liveness risk, handled by added messages.

questionText: In the Ricart-Agrawala distributed mutual-exclusion algorithm, to enter the critical section a process must:
options: Receive REPLY messages from all other processes
Get permission from a central coordinator
explanation: Ricart-Agrawala is a permission-based algorithm: a requester broadcasts a timestamped request and may enter the CS only after receiving REPLYs from every other process. It uses 2(N−1) messages per CS entry.
distractorRationales: A circulating token is the token-ring approach, not Ricart-Agrawala.
A central coordinator is the centralized algorithm, a different scheme.
Timeouts do not grant CS access; explicit replies do.

questionText: The Bully election algorithm elects as leader the live process with the:
explanation: In the Bully algorithm, when a process notices the coordinator failed, it starts an election; the highest-ID responsive process 'bullies' the others and becomes the new coordinator.
distractorRationales: The Bully algorithm chooses the highest ID, not the lowest.
Timestamps are not the selection criterion; process IDs are.
Message counts do not determine the elected leader.
